title: The Glory of the Gospel Ministry
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Glory of the Gospel Ministry_ 2 Corinthians 4-1-9 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: We have this ministry by mercy — we do not lose heart (v.1)
Renouncing shameful and crafty handling of God’s Word (v.2)
We preach Christ Jesus as Lord, not ourselves (v.5)
God’s light shining in our hearts to give the light of Christ (v.6)
Treasure in jars of clay — the glory belongs to God, not us (v.7)
Hard pressed but not crushed; struck down but not destroyed (vv.8-9)
summary: Exposition of 2 Corinthians 4:1-9 in the ongoing 2 Corinthians series. Paul’s gospel ministry is carried on with mercy-given perseverance, absolute integrity in handling God’s Word, and Christ-centered proclamation. The great paradox is that we are jars of clay carrying the priceless treasure of the gospel—hard pressed, perplexed, persecuted, and struck down—yet never crushed, despairing, abandoned, or destroyed, because the surpassing power belongs to God.
transcript:
This letter, as we have been seeing, is highly personal and highly autobiographical. We can learn a lot about Paul from this letter. Paul is answering several personal attacks that have been brought against him, but it is important that we realize he is not answering them merely to vindicate himself. He is answering them because the kinds of attacks brought against him are the kinds that would discredit the gospel of Jesus Christ — because often as the ministry is discredited, so the message is discredited.

He answers the complaints from the supposed super-apostles who had come into the church and said they were better than Paul: that Paul did not speak well, that he did not use big words, that he was not trained in Greek rhetoric, that he was not a good orator, that he was too simple. These are the accusations being brought against Paul, and he answers them because they affect the Corinthians' understanding of the gospel itself.

What Paul does is pull back the curtain on his life and let us see his calling, his conscience, his confidence, and his Christ in these six verses.

## His Calling: A Ministry Received by Mercy

Look at verse 1: *"Therefore, since we have this ministry, as we have received mercy, we do not lose heart."*

The "we" and "our" throughout the passage refers to Paul and Timothy — we know this by looking back at chapter 1:1, where it says Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God, and Timothy our brother. So all the "we's" and "our's" we find here refer to Paul and Timothy.

Paul says this ministry — the new covenant ministry we saw in chapter 3, a ministry of surpassing and permanent glory, visible glory, liberating glory, and transforming glory — Paul has it, and he knows it is from the Lord, because it is a ministry of mercy. And because of that, he does not lose heart. The expression "do not lose heart" is a way of saying: because this ministry came to us from the mercy of God, we don't quit. It is not based on the performance of the people he ministered to, nor on his own performance, but on the performance of Christ and his mercy.

That sentence literally translates: "while having this ministry, we were shown mercy." Mercy and ministry are happening at the same time. This ministry was given in the form of mercy. Paul did not earn this calling. God showed him mercy, and that is how he was called to minister to the Corinthians.

Paul retells his testimony at least three times in the New Testament. We have the original account in Acts 9, then in Acts 22 when he preaches on the stairs of the Roman fortress, and again in Acts 26 before Herod Agrippa, hoping he would come to Christ — to which Agrippa replied, "You almost persuade me to become a Christian."

In Acts 26:12–18, Paul recounts how, while journeying to Damascus with authority from the chief priests, at midday he saw a light from heaven brighter than the sun, and heard a voice saying in Hebrew, "Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It is hard for you to kick against the goads." And he replied, "Who are you, Lord?" And the Lord said, "I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting. But rise and stand on your feet, for I have appeared to you for this purpose, to make you a minister and a witness... I will deliver you from the Jewish people and from the Gentiles, to whom I now send you, to open their eyes, in order to turn them from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and an inheritance among those who are sanctified by faith in me."

This man who was persecuting Christ, knocked to the ground and knowing there was nothing good in him — that is when God picks him up and says, "You are going to be my servant." This mercy was what launched Paul and Timothy into ministry in Corinth. And this mercy was also shown to them in the form of a clean conscience.

So Paul and Timothy persevered because the ministry was not self-created but God-given. Brothers and sisters, the commitment Paul demonstrates to the ministry is an encouragement for us to persevere in the calling with which God has called us. Don't lose heart — whatever God called you to do, even if obstacles are being thrown your way. Your calling may be as a husband or wife, as parent or child, as employer or employee, as single, or as a brother and sister in Christ. If God called you to that position, He will sustain you. As Paul says in Galatians 6:9, *"Let us not grow weary while doing good, for in due season we shall reap if we do not lose heart."*

## His Conscience: Clear Before God and Man

Look at verse 2: *"But we have renounced the hidden things of shame, not walking in craftiness, nor handling the word of God deceitfully, but by manifestation of the truth, commending ourselves to every man's conscience in the sight of God."*

Paul lists what he refused to do in his ministry among the Corinthians: no cunning, no deceit, no disgraceful or underhanded ways, no tampering with God's word. Perhaps these were the accusations being made against him, and he says the evidence shows the opposite. Or perhaps this is what the false apostles were actually doing, and Paul is showing the Corinthians he is not one of them.

What a glorious thing to not have anything to hide — to know you have done what God calls you to do, to have a clear conscience before God and man. Paul writes to Timothy later in 1 Timothy 1:5: *"The purpose of the commandment is love from a pure heart, from a good conscience, and from sincere faith."* This clear conscience comes ultimately from the blood of Christ, who cleanses us from all sin. Hebrews 9:14 says: *"How much more shall the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered Himself without spot to God, cleanse your conscience from dead works to serve the living God."*

If you have an unclean conscience, the only way to cleanse it is through the blood of the Lord Jesus Christ. Paul has a clear conscience because his ministry strategy is rooted in truth. Being truthful is glorious because it means you do not have to remember anything. When you tell the truth, you simply say what is true. When you lie, you have to keep track of every lie to keep the story straight. Paul stands before the Corinthians and before God with a clear conscience because he dedicates himself to the truth of God. He does not manipulate the gospel — he simply proclaims it.

Brothers and sisters, we do not need cleverness in our lives. We need clarity and truth, especially in relationships.

## His Confidence: The Gospel of Jesus Christ

Look at verses 3 and 4: *"But even if our gospel is veiled, it is veiled to those who are perishing, whose minds the god of this age has blinded, who do not believe, lest the light of the gospel of the glory of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine on them."*

His confidence is the gospel of Jesus Christ. One of the accusations the false apostles made against Paul was that he was not easy to understand, that he was not skilled with words. Paul acknowledges this in 1 Corinthians 2:1–5 — yes, he has not been trained in Greek rhetoric; he speaks plainly. But the reason people cannot understand the gospel is not his plain speech. It is because their hearts are veiled and they are spiritually blinded.

Notice that the word "perishing" in verse 3 is a present participle — it describes an ongoing, active condition. Those who refuse to believe in the Lord Jesus Christ are perishing right now. You are not merely in danger of future destruction. You are being destroyed now, by your refusal to believe. In 2 Thessalonians 2:9–10, using the same form, Paul describes those who perish "because they did not receive the love of the truth that they might be saved."

Friend, if you are walking apart from the Lord, you are perishing. You are not improving yourself. You are not going to a better place.

Satan — the god of this age — blinds unbelievers through false ideas about identity and through distractions. The 17th-century pastor Thomas Brooks wrote a still-relevant book called *Precious Remedies Against Satan's Devices*, showing how Satan uses false ideas about who we are, cultural influences, distractions, and pride to keep people from seeing Christ. Jesus himself warned in Matthew 24:24 that false christs and false prophets would rise to deceive, if possible, even the elect.

Satan does not want you to see Christ, because to see Christ is to see God. Christ is the image of God. If we want to see God, we need to look at Jesus. That is why the god of Judaism apart from Christ, the god of Islam, and the god of the Mormons are not the God of the Bible. The God of the Bible can only be known through the Son — who is God himself in the flesh, who became like one of us, lived a perfect life, died the death of separation from the Father that you and I deserve, rose from the dead, and reigns over his church.

When Scripture says Christ is the image of God, this is not an invitation to make statues of Jesus. It is a call to know God through Jesus — which is the only way to know God. As Jesus said in John 14: *"I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me."* And as Hebrews 1:3 says, Christ is *"the brightness of His glory and the express image of His person."* Paul writes in Colossians 1:15: *"He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation."*

This means evangelism is not merely intellectual persuasion. If you are here today and do not believe in Jesus, it is not because you have an intellectual objection. The mind that believes in Jesus and the mind under the power of sin that does not believe are both the same fallen mind. This is not intellectual warfare. It is spiritual warfare. Only God can remove the veil from your eyes that you might see Jesus.

## His Christ: Not Ourselves but Christ

Look at verses 5 and 6: *"For we do not preach ourselves, but Christ Jesus the Lord, and ourselves your bondservants for Jesus' sake. For it is the God who commanded light to shine out of darkness who has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ."*

Paul's ministry model was not "ourselves" but Christ. The false apostles were promoting themselves, but true ministry of the gospel proclaims Christ. New covenant ministry is not personality-driven, platform-driven, or ego-driven. It is Christ-driven. The minister of the new covenant is a servant of the people entrusted to his care — not the other way around. That is why they are called pastors, meaning shepherds. Their authority is exercised through service.

And there is an application for every Christian here: your life should say, "Not me, but Christ." Is that what your life is saying? Or are you the center of your own little planetary system? Is your life saying, "Not me, but Christ"?

In verse 6, do you hear the echo of Genesis 1:3–4? *"Then God said, 'Let there be light,' and there was light."* The same God who created physical light by speaking creates spiritual light by speaking as well. In creation, God spoke into the darkness and light appeared. In salvation, God speaks into dead hearts and life appears. As Paul says in Ephesians 2:10, the regenerated person is *"God's workmanship, created in Christ Jesus unto good works."*

## Three Applications

**First, do not lose heart in faithful service.** Because your service to Christ is based on mercy, not merit, you do not have to quit when people do not respond to you, when your work feels unnoticed, when you face criticism, or when results seem slow. You may see this in parents discipling their children — saying the same things again and again with no visible change. Don't lose heart. God has appointed you as that minister, and change comes by the Spirit working in the hearts of your children. Keep discipling them. We see this also in Christians sharing the gospel with friends — we don't quit, because it is not our performance but the Spirit of God at work. Measure faithfulness, not results.

**Second, practice transparent integrity.** A Christian life and ministry must be marked by honesty, transparency, and clear speech, not manipulation. This applies to business, social media, teaching Scripture, and relationships. We do not bend truth to make it more attractive. We present it plainly and trust God with the outcome. Truth does not need gimmicks.

**Third, remember that spiritual blindness is real.** When people reject Christ, do not take it personally — they are simply acting according to their nature. Do not be shaken in your faith. Do not respond with anger. Instead, pray that the Lord would shine light upon them, show patience, and maintain compassion. They are perishing people. Evangelism is not merely intellectual persuasion — it is the work of divine illumination.

With Paul, we do not lose heart: our ministry comes from mercy, our conscience is clean through Christ, the blindness of others is not our failure, our message is Christ and not ourselves, and the God who created light creates faith. We persevere in our homes, in our church, in our workplaces, and in our witness — because the same God who said, "Let there be light," is saying to the darkest heart, "Let there be light." And when He does, they will see the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.
